design: design:builtin — BUILT-IN FORMS MAXIMALIST: ~30 compiler-known TCB forms + normative form table, 9 taught compositions, closed derived hash/eq/ord, closed 3-idiom atomics, classify64/view schemas
lens: PERFORMANCE HOLES — concrete common workloads where no blessed writing plausibly reaches the named SOTA; performance predictability; whether form contracts yield exploitable optimizer facts

findings.title: No shared-ownership / hot-swap snapshot primitive: read-mostly non-keyed shared state cannot reach arc-swap/RCU par
findings.detail: Scenario: config hot-reload, routing tables, compiled rulesets, feature-flag trees — every long-running server rebuilds a large owned structure while N threads read it millions of times/sec. SOTA is arc-swap/RCU: read = one acquire load (~2 ns), zero shared-line writes, linear core scaling. The catalog has NO spelling: pubcell<T> requires T:Copy (a vec-backed trie is not Copy); snapmap_get returns values by move/copy, so a multi-KB Config cannot be read through it; box<T> is unique, borrows cannot be stored, and there is no Arc-class refcounted or epoch-guarded shared-read form. The only legal spelling is mutex<Config>, whose reader line-bouncing plateau at 2-3 threads is exactly the fail line the design's own coverage map names for the sharded-map scenario. scoped-spawn borrows cover the static case only — replacement-while-serving is impossible because the borrow region must enclose all readers. Evidence: microbench a config-reload server (8 reader threads, 1 Hz writer) in the best blessed spelling vs Rust arc-swap; expect 5-50x read-path deficit and negative scaling past ~4 cores. Fix is additive and cheap within the stance: one 'snapcell<T>' form (epoch-guarded region-scoped borrow of the current snapshot, publish-by-move), or a borrow-returning snapmap_get; the digest as written has neither.

findings.title: No lane-arithmetic SIMD: every numeric/columnar hot loop is 4-16x off par, and the PARTIAL self-assessment badly understates the breadth
findings.detail: classify64's stage algebra is byte-in, bitmask-out (nibble shuffles, byte range/eq compares, clmul, movemask). It has no 32/64-bit or float lanes, no lane multiplies/adds beyond saturating byte arithmetic, no horizontal reductions, no gather, no compress-store. That excludes: analytical column filters producing selection vectors (DuckDB-class i32/f64 predicate scans run 10-20 GB/s with AVX2 compare+vpcompressd; scalar ANF with match per element runs ~1-3 GB/s even with bounds elided), dot products / distance kernels (embedding search — the hottest loop in current infrastructure, 8-16x SIMD speedup), pixel/DSP loops, AoS-to-SoA interleave transforms, hex/UTF-16 transcoding. The design frames the gap as 'novel codecs like dav1d needing GFNI' but the demand map's F9-1 is high-weight precisely because ALL of F5 plus databases sit on it. Fallback is backend auto-vectorization of match-based ANF loops — reliable only for straight reductions (the measured 3.3x checked-law case); shuffle-, selection-, and mixed-width kernels do not auto-vectorize in LLVM today from scalar loops. Evidence: implement (a) i32 > const selection-vector kernel and (b) f32x dot product in blessed forms, asm-inspect and bench vs highway/std::simd; predict >=4x deficit on (a), >=8x on (b). This is the single widest common-scenario hole and the 'catalog finding' remedy (ship one named kernel per request) cannot track an open-ended numeric surface.

findings.title: pool<T> handle dereference tax breaks the claimed 10-20% band on linked-structure traversal, with no elision mechanism and no safe fallback
findings.detail: Every hop in the taught tree/graph/intrusive-links cards is pool_get(h) = bounds check + generation compare + Option construction + mandatory match arm (ANF, match-only). Against the baseline the coverage claims (C++ pointer DOM, or rowan/indextree which use unchecked or bounds-only u32 indices in hot paths), that is +2-4 cycles and +1 branch on a 4-6 cycle L1-resident hop: 30-60% overhead, not the promised 10-20%, on DOM edits, AST navigation with parent links, timer-wheel bucket walks, and LRU unlink chains (the lru-parity claim inherits this on every link touch). The requires/facts machinery is specified for bounds only — nothing can discharge a generation check, and the checker cannot know the handle wasn't stale. The escape (plain vec<T> + u32, no generations) exists only for append-only structures (P2); churn-heavy structures would have to hand-roll slot recycling with enum Free/Occupied slots, paying the same tag check and reintroducing exactly the recycled-index-aliasing hazard the project rejected. Also: no split-borrow on pool means two-node mutations (relink, rotate, swap) cannot hold two &uniq nodes — remove/reinsert or field-copy dances add stores per structural edit. Evidence: 1M-hop L1-resident tree walk and a DOM-edit churn bench, pool-handle spelling vs Rust indextree (bounds-only) and C++ pointers; falsified if within 20% of the pointer baseline.

findings.title: The iteration/inlining contract is unspecified, and the flagship PAR claims silently assume full intrinsic lowering plus Option-match vectorization
findings.detail: Bulk scans are the workload: 'sum 1M u64 at ~1 elem/cycle' requires iter_next(&uniq it) -> Option<T> to (a) inline-lower from the form table rather than call into an opaque TCB kernel, and (b) have the loop{match Some/None} shape recognized and vectorized. The spec digest defines op TYPING by table lookup but never says which ops are inline-lowered intrinsics vs out-of-line kernel calls. vec is plausibly intrinsic; map iteration (SwissTable control-byte scan, ~10 instructions of state machine), omap range iteration (B-tree leaf walk), and pool live-slot iteration are not single intrinsics — if they compile as a call per element, that is ~5-8 ns/elem vs hashbrown's ~1-2 ns/elem inline, a 3-5x loss on iterate-heavy workloads (compiler passes iterating symbol tables, cache sweeps, serialization of whole maps). Compounding: the op lists omit bulk mutators — no retain/drain on vec or map, no extend-from-iterator, no map merge — so predicate eviction becomes iterate + collect keys into a temp vec + remove-per-key: one allocation plus a second full probe pass, >=2x vs hashbrown retain's single pass. E1 as designed (push-then-sum on vec) cannot catch this. Evidence: asm-diff of map-iterate-sum and a retain-equivalent sweep vs hashbrown 0.15; the spec needs an explicit per-op lowering column (intrinsic vs kernel-call) before any PAR claim over iteration is credible.

findings.title: Closed derived-order keys: argsort and computed keys have no direct spelling — undermining the design's own SoA substrate and part of the sort WIN
findings.detail: sort_by_key accepts only field paths / derived orders. The canonical SoA operation — sort a u32 index permutation by values in a parallel column (P2 is the project's OWN blessed substrate; every columnar engine and compiler pass does this) — is not a field path on the sorted elements, so the blessed spelling is materialize (key, idx) pairs into a fresh vec, sort, extract: one extra allocation plus 2x memory traffic, ~1.3-1.6x off Rust sort_unstable_by on the same shape (and Rust's sort_by_cached_key when keys are expensive). Computed keys (order by price*qty, by |x|, by a decoded be-u32 inside a record — SQLite record comparators) all take the same materialization detour. The radix WIN itself is real but narrower than claimed: it holds for primitive keys with small payloads; struct sorts with >=32B payloads become bandwidth-bound on the multi-pass moves and fall back to comparison anyway. Evidence: bench argsort of 10M u32 indices by an f64 column, blessed pair-materialization vs Rust sort_unstable_by; and rerun E1's radix threshold with 48B payload elements.

findings.title: iobuf's memset-avoidance excludes writer-authored producers; backpatching serializers pay double stores
findings.detail: Only TCB endpoints (file/net/evring, codec kit) and push_slice may extend iobuf's filled prefix. A writer-authored decoder or generator (custom RLE/delta decoder, columnar decompressor outside the shipped LZ kit, image unpacker) therefore cannot write computed values into unfilled capacity: it either pre-fills (memset 64KB ≈ 2 us + evicting 64KB of cache — the exact tax the form exists to remove) or pushes element-wise through vec (len store + capacity branch per element; the branch is requires-elidable but the len update and the loss of the plain store-loop shape remain). Similarly, view-based mutable emission requires initialized bytes, so length-prefix backpatching serializers (protobuf submessages, flatbuffers) zero-fill then overwrite: 2x store traffic on the emit path vs C/unsafe-Rust writers. Impact is bounded (~20-50% on affected decode/emit paths, which are common in exactly the database/network audience). Evidence: writer-authored RLE decoder producing 64KB blocks, blessed spelling vs C; count zeroing stores in codegen — the design's own 'zero memset instructions' CI pin fails for user-authored producers.

findings.title: Postcondition-fact channel contradicts the flow-insensitive checker: most claimed intra-function elisions may never fire
findings.detail: The coverage story leans on facts (sort establishes sortedness consumed by later binary_search; result <= len elides follow-up indexing; filled() <= capacity). But FN-8/requires is an entry-prologue mechanism, and the checker story explicitly promises 'no flow-sensitive reasoning beyond OWN-1..13'. Consuming a sort-op postcondition at a search five statements later requires precisely the intra-function fact dataflow (with airtight invalidation on every intervening &uniq access — including statement-scoped minted borrows, which touch the binding on EVERY op) that the settled frontend-simplicity ruling forbids. Either the facts are consumable only across function boundaries via requires clauses — in which case straight-line sort-then-search code keeps its checks and the accounting behind several PAR verdicts quietly loses a few percent — or the checker grows real flow sensitivity, reopening the feasibility ruling. Per-check cost is small (a predicted branch per probe level), but the design bills these facts as a load-bearing optimizer channel and they are also the designated hostile-review surface. Evidence: prototype the checker rule and test whether sort(v); binary_search(&v,k) in one function elides the search's residual bounds check; specify invalidation semantics in the table and count which coverage claims survive if the answer is no.

findings.title: Silent knob cliffs: par depends on non-checkable workload knowledge (INLINE, spsc-vs-chan, map-vs-omap) and E3 never tests knob selection
findings.detail: The catalog's PAR verdicts assume the writer picks the right variant, but the wrong-but-safe choices are silent 2-5x cliffs: vec/map INLINE=0 default on an LLVM-shaped millions-of-tiny-sequences workload loses the entire 3-5x SmallVector win (the design's own par target); chan where spsc fits is 3-5x (acknowledged as only 'a taught lint' — no lint mechanism is specified, and detecting SPSC-eligibility from source requires whole-program endpoint reasoning the checker doesn't do); omap for point-only lookups is 2-3x; pool where arena fits adds the generation tax to every access. Decision tables route on contract ('need order?'), not on workload ('allocation-dominated? how many entries typically?'), which is exactly what a modest writer cannot know a priori and the design provides no profiling loop or advisory diagnostics to recover. E3 tests whether a sonnet-class writer composes cards correctly, not whether it selects knobs — so the experiment can pass while typical generated code sits 2-4x off the catalog's pinned targets. Evidence: extend E3 with 10 knob-sensitive tasks (tiny-map compiler workload, two-thread pipeline, point-lookup table) scoring the perf ratio of the chosen form vs the optimal form; predict the median chosen/optimal ratio lands near 2x without added routing guidance.

overall: The stance is rescuable: the form-table architecture, TCB kernel discipline, and the container/queue/I-O coverage largely survive this lens — vec/map/sort/spsc/evring claims are credible as machine shapes, and the affine-endpoint and radix-dispatch WINs are real if narrower than advertised. The genuine holes cluster where the catalog's closedness meets open-ended computation: (1) no lane-arithmetic SIMD leaves every numeric/columnar/distance hot loop 4-16x off par — the widest common-scenario deficit and the single change that matters most is shipping a highway-class width-explicit SIMD value-type cluster (with the same closed-vocabulary discipline as the atomics), not more named kernels; (2) one missing shared-snapshot form locks read-mostly hot-swap state onto a mutex plateau; (3) the pool generation tax and the unspecified iteration-inlining contract both sit directly under existing PAR claims and must be pinned by asm-level evidence, not asserted. The minor findings (argsort detour, writer-side memset boundary, fact-channel flow contradiction, knob cliffs) are individually survivable but collectively describe the same failure mode: the design's par is conditional on the writer and the optimizer each doing something the spec never guarantees. Fix the SIMD gap, add the snapshot form, and write the lowering/fact-flow contracts into the table before E1-E3 are allowed to claim anything.

design: design:builtin — BUILT-IN FORMS MAXIMALIST: ~30 compiler-known opaque forms (containers, byte/SIMD kernels, concurrency, I/O) with TCB internals, a normative form table, and 9 taught compositions over an essentially unchanged 90-rule kernel.
lens: SAFETY & TCB — forgeable authority at API boundaries, aliasing/invalidation across form ops, ownership in/out double-consumption, concurrent publication/reclamation contracts, TCB inventory honesty, and whether the new checker judgments are actually frontend-simple.

findings.title: aref<'r,T> is Copy + get_uniq: two &uniq aliases to the same allocation, or the arena pattern stops working — pick one
findings.detail: aref is Copy, storable in data, nonforgeable, and derefs with zero runtime checks. Take the design's own tree shape: `let x = arena_alloc(&a, e); let y = x;` (Copy duplication is legal and is the entire point — parent and child structs both store the aref). Now `region 'g { let p: &uniq Expr = arena_get_uniq(&'g a, x); let q: &uniq Expr = arena_get_uniq(&'g a, y); }`. If get_uniq takes the arena SHARED (as arena_alloc's shown `&a` signature does), nothing in the four stated checker judgments prevents p and q from being live simultaneously: two &uniq to the same T. That is not just a logic bug — &uniq is the optimizer's noalias fact (the scoped-alias channel is a measured 17x win in this project), so aliased &uniq is a miscompilation vector: writes through p can be reordered/cached past reads through q. T2 dies in fully 'checked' writer code with no TCB bug anywhere. The only local fix — get_uniq takes &uniq arena, serializing all derefs through one statement/region-scoped exclusive hold of the whole arena — destroys the advertised pattern: you cannot hold &uniq to a parent node while calling arena_alloc for its child (alloc needs the arena too), so 'same-arena cross-references solved' collapses to a one-live-reference-at-a-time discipline nobody's compiler AST pass can live with. EVIDENCE TO SETTLE: write the form-table signatures for arena_alloc/get/get_uniq and produce a typing derivation that (a) rejects the p/q program above AND (b) admits rustc-style build-tree-then-mutate-node code. I claim no signature satisfies both without adding per-aref uniqueness tracking, which is Rust-scale inference the checker feasibility ruling forbids.

findings.title: Arena reset-and-reuse: the 'fresh inner region per cycle' brand does not exist in the stated typing, so a cycle-1 aref derefs recycled memory with zero checks
findings.detail: arefs are branded by the arena's region: arena<'r> mints aref<'r,T>. Reset-and-reuse is claimed sound 'where the region system proves no aref survives (new inner region per cycle)'. But an inner region 'i cannot brand the arefs unless arena_alloc's result is branded by something OTHER than the arena's own region — i.e., alloc must be parameterized over a fresh region per cycle and the arena must be re-brandable after reset. That is a new mechanism (region-indexed re-branding of a live value), absent from the language_deltas and from the four checker judgments. As specified: `region 'r { let a = arena_new(); loop { let x: aref<'r,Node> = arena_alloc(&a, n); stash.push(x); arena_reset(&uniq a); } }` — x is branded 'r, 'r is still live, stash is ordinary data holding Copy values, and the post-reset deref of x is a zero-check load from memory the bump pointer has re-issued to a different type. Well-typed UAF plus type confusion in writer code — exactly the bug class the pool's REJECTED free-list alternative was thrown out for, reintroduced through the arena's back door. EVIDENCE TO SETTLE: exhibit the reset op's table signature and the kernel rule that rejects the program above. If reset requires the arena itself to be consumed and re-minted under a new region (the only sound shape I can see), say so — and then explain how retained-capacity reuse works when arena_new can't see the old chunks.

findings.title: Iterator freeze and the entry API require loan tracking the checker story doesn't admit; as written, the design's own source shapes either don't type-check or admit rehash-invalidated interior pointers
findings.detail: Two rules collide. (1) Statement-scoped minting: any form op on a 'uniquely-held binding' mints a borrow that dies at statement end — so `map_insert(&uniq m, k2, v2)` is always available while m is a local. (2) Form iterators hold a 'hidden borrow' and the container 'is frozen until the iterator's region ends'. Who enforces the freeze? For `region 'x { let it = map_iter(&'x m); loop { match iter_next(&uniq it) {...}; map_insert(&uniq m, k2, v2); } }` to be rejected, the checker must know m is loaned to a live region value — a per-binding loan set with region-keyed release. That is a borrow checker (a small one, but new ownership machinery), directly contradicting 'no new ownership machinery' and the 'four local judgments' claim. If it is NOT tracked, the insert rehashes the SwissTable and iter_next's interior pointer walks freed control bytes: memory corruption in checked code. Worse, the entry API cannot satisfy the minting rule at all: `match map_entry(&uniq m, k) { Vacant(slot) => map_insert_at(slot, 0) }` — slot is a borrow-carrying value living INSIDE an enum (violating no-borrows-in-data) and it survives past the entry statement into the match arm (violating statement-scoped death); any op on m between entry and insert_at (trivial to write in an arm) leaves slot dangling across a rehash. EVIDENCE TO SETTLE: formalize the minting + iterator-region rules and machine-check three programs: (a) push;push (must pass), (b) iterate-while-insert (must fail), (c) the design's own entry source shape (must pass). I predict no rule set in the stated 'lookup + structural recursion' complexity class passes all three; you need loan sets, and the design should own that honestly — it is decidable (lexical regions, borrows can't escape data or calls), but it is a fifth judgment and it is flow-sensitive.

findings.title: sendable(T) as specified classifies aref-bearing data as sendable: cross-thread arena access with zero-check derefs
findings.detail: The sendable judgment: 'owned and containing no thread-affine form, or a borrow whose region encloses the join'. aref<'r,T> is by explicit design 'data, not a borrow', it is Copy, and it is not a form instance — a struct { n: aref<'r,Node> } is owned data containing no thread-affine form, hence sendable through scoped spawn, chan<T>, or pubcell<T:Copy>. Thread B then calls arena get/get_uniq... no wait — B needs &arena to deref, and the arena itself only crosses as a region-enclosing borrow in scoped spawn. So the concrete hole is scoped spawn: 'r encloses the join, both the &arena borrow and aref copies cross legally, and now two threads deref (and get_uniq-mutate, per finding 1) the same arena memory with no sync form involved, while any same-scope arena_alloc (&a, shared!) races the bump pointer against readers. For chan/pubcell the aref crosses but the arena doesn't — a stashed aref outliving its usefulness is then a latent cross-thread UAF the moment any future API lets an arena reference cross. The fix is easy but must be stated as a rule, not an intention: aref<'r,T> (and any region-branded token: iterators, views, cursors, mmap slices, guards) is sendable ONLY under the same region-encloses-join test as borrows, and forms reachable through a crossed borrow must be sync-flagged for shared ops (arena is not). EVIDENCE TO SETTLE: the sendable structural-recursion definition written out over the actual type grammar, plus a test program shipping struct{aref} through pubcell — it must be rejected, and today's text does not reject it.

findings.title: condvar wait aliases &uniq State across the park: the guard borrow must be consumed and re-minted, or noalias facts miscompile the wait loop
findings.detail: mutex<T> grants a region-scoped &uniq T guard with compiler-derived unlock at region end, and 'condvar wait on the guard'. Condvar wait semantically unlocks, parks, and relocks — during the park another thread holds &uniq to the SAME State and mutates it. If the waiter's &uniq is lexically continuous across the wait call (region 'g still open, binding s still live), the checker sees an unbroken unique borrow and the optimizer is entitled to noalias-cache fields of State in registers across the call — the waiter then re-tests a predicate against a stale value and sleeps forever or proceeds on garbage state. This is a real, known compiler bug class (it is why Rust's Condvar::wait takes the guard BY VALUE and returns a new one). The sound spelling under xlang's affine rules is exactly that: `s2 = condvar_wait(cv, move s)` — consume the borrow, mint a fresh one — and it composes fine with no-reborrow. But the design says 'condvar wait on the guard' without this shape, and the unlock-at-region-end mechanism itself is novel machinery (a borrow whose region close emits a runtime effect — borrows currently have no drop semantics). EVIDENCE TO SETTLE: the table signature of condvar_wait. If the guard parameter mode is & or &uniq rather than own-in/own-out, the form is unsound as specified; also specify what happens if the writer's region ends (derived unlock) while a condvar_wait re-lock is pending after a trap.

findings.title: evring abandonment: affine buffers can be loaned to the kernel forever — ring drop with in-flight ops means the kernel writes into freed iobufs unless an unstated blocking drop protocol is in the TCB, and that protocol is outside the entire five-part verification story
findings.detail: The loan-through-completion rule types the happy path (submit moves iobuf in, completion moves it out). Affine ownership also permits ABANDONMENT: the writer submits a read on a stalled fd, breaks out of the harvest loop, and lets the ring go out of scope; compiler-derived drop frees the ring and its owned iobufs. io_uring semantics: the kernel still owns the submitted SQE and will complete it later, writing into the (now freed, possibly reallocated) buffer — a kernel-mediated UAF that no userspace typing can see. The TCB drop must therefore: cancel all in-flight ops (IORING_OP_ASYNC_CANCEL is itself async and can fail on ops in uninterruptible states), then BLOCK until every completion is harvested — potentially unboundedly (NFS hang, pipe peer gone). Same class: deterministic trap/abort while ops are in flight (abort is fine only if the process dies before any free — must be guaranteed, including under a trap-in-another-thread), and scoped-thread parent trapping while children hold stack borrows (join-before-unwind must be structural). None of this is falsifiable by the stated verification: differential tests and GenMC/loom model checking are userspace-schedule tools; they cannot exercise kernel completion-after-free timing. EVIDENCE TO SETTLE: (a) the specified drop semantics of evring with nonzero in-flight count, in the form table's trap/effect columns; (b) a fault-injection harness (stalled fd + drop + immediate heap churn under ASAN on the C-compiled kernels) added to the verification plan. If the answer is 'drop leaks the buffers instead of freeing' — fine, but then say so and reconcile it with 'all drops are compiler-derived'.

findings.title: The classify64/view lowerers are compilers-in-the-TCB whose input is adversarial writer source, but the verification story only validates the shipped instances
findings.detail: classify64 and view are the two 'checked source' bright spots, but their LOWERERS take arbitrary writer-authored schemas (any stage DAG over shuffles/ranges/clmul/masks; any field layout with offsets/widths/alignment) and emit per-ISA machine code. A lowerer bug is a miscompile reachable from ordinary unprivileged source — e.g., a stage combination whose AVX-512 lowering gets a mask-width promotion wrong and reads a 64B pshufb table as 32B (OOB read baked into 'safe' code), or a view with a width/alignment pair whose NEON path emits an unchecked unaligned atomic-width load. The tcb section's five-part plan verifies KERNELS (map, spsc, etc.) and the four shipped classify64 instances — it never mentions fuzzing the schema space or differential-executing writer-authored kernels against a scalar reference semantics. For view, the design even has the right tool (asm-diff CI) but pins it only to C-parity performance, not to a safety property. This is the same epistemic hole as 'the compiler is TCB' — except the compiler's input grammar is 90 rules old and battle-tested, while these two grammars are new and reverse-engineered from three examples. EVIDENCE TO SETTLE: add and run (a) a scalar reference interpreter for the stage algebra + differential fuzzing of random well-formed schemas across all four ISA lowerings, (b) bounds-annotated codegen checks (every emitted table load provably within the declared table object). If the team balks at building the reference interpreter, that is itself the finding: the schema is not really checked source.

findings.title: Postcondition-fact invalidation is named as a risk but has no semantics: which ops kill which facts, and what happens across helper calls, is exactly where a stale fact becomes silent check elision
findings.detail: Facts enter from the form table (sortedness from sort, result<=len from binary_search, filled<=cap from iobuf) and feed the FN-8 requires/elision machinery — a channel that ELIDES REAL CHECKS. The table must therefore also specify a kill set, and the design never does. Concrete stale-fact programs: (1) sort(&uniq v) establishes sorted(v); vec_push(&uniq v, 0) appends an unsorted tail — does push kill sorted(v)? (2) sorted(v) held, then `helper(&uniq v)` where helper is ordinary writer code that stores through indices — interprocedurally, EVERY &uniq escape must kill every fact on the binding (fine, but say it, and note it also kills the fact across the statement-scoped mints of finding 3, which happen implicitly on every op). (3) facts on slices vs facts on the owning vec: sort ran on as_slice_uniq's view — does the fact attach to the view (dead at region end, useless) or the vec (then aliasing between view-facts and container-facts needs rules)? The failure mode is not wrong answers: if a fact like `result<=len` or `filled<=capacity` is consumed to elide a bounds check after an op that shrank the container (truncate after binary_search's cached result), the elided check is an OOB write in checked code. Fact channels get hostile review by standing rule; this one arrives with its invalidation column blank. EVIDENCE TO SETTLE: the form table's fact column extended with an explicit kill/preserve column per op, plus adversarial programs (sort; truncate; use-stale-index) run through the prototype's elision pass showing the check is NOT elided.

findings.title: pool<T> 32-bit generation wrap is reachable in minutes on a LIFO-hot slot; the design treats stale-handle confusion as a documented hazard but wrap silently converts 'detectable' into 'undetectable'
findings.detail: The freelist is LIFO, so a steady insert/remove churn reuses ONE slot; at the quoted 2-5 ns per insert+remove cycle, a tight loop wraps a 32-bit generation in 2^32 * ~4 ns ≈ 17 seconds; a realistic server doing 1M churn ops/s on a small pool wraps a hot slot in ~1.2 hours. After wrap, a retained stale handle passes the generation check and get/get_uniq/remove return a LIVE, WRONG object — including remove() moving out a T that other code still holds handles to. Memory-safe (T1 holds — the slot is live), but the form's advertised contract 'stale handles yield None, never misbehavior' is quietly false at scale, and the safety-keystone marketing invites writers to treat handles as capabilities they are not. Cheap falsification/fix: (a) a churn microbench demonstrating wrap-and-alias in under an hour; (b) either widen to a 64-bit generation (slotmap's own choice for keys is 32/32 in 64 bits total — wrap analysis should be pinned in the form's contract), or randomize/never-reuse generations and state the actual bound in the table entry rather than 'never misbehavior'.

overall: The stance is rescuable — the core bet (SOTA kernels behind affine, opaque form APIs; concurrency structure typed by endpoint ownership; no user comparators) is sound and the spsc/evring/par safety arguments are genuinely better than convention-based C++/Rust. But the design currently has one load-bearing lie and one load-bearing omission. The lie: 'no new ownership machinery / four local judgments' — iterators, entry slots, guards, and fill_buf slices all require a per-binding loan set (a small lexical borrow checker), and pretending otherwise leaves the rules either rejecting the design's own source shapes or admitting rehash-invalidated interior pointers. The omission: the arena. aref's combination of Copy + storable + zero-check deref + get_uniq is the single place where writer-reachable code can mint aliased &uniq and post-reset dangling access, i.e., where T1/T2 break without any TCB bug; findings 1 and 2 are both instances of the arena granting authority (unique access, liveness) that no checker judgment actually tracks. The one change that matters most: redesign the arena so aref never confers unchecked authority — either brand-per-cycle arenas with own-in/own-out reset and get_uniq gated by exclusive arena hold with an explicit story for the build-while-referencing pattern, or fall back to runtime-checked arena derefs (pool-style) and let requires-prologue facts earn the checks back. Do that, own the fifth (loan-tracking) judgment honestly, and the remaining findings — sendable over region-branded tokens, condvar guard consumption, evring drop protocol, lowerer fuzzing, fact kill-sets — are hard but ordinary specification-and-verification work rather than structural flaws.

findings.title: The 36-rule estimate undercounts the normative surface by roughly 5-8x; the honest form table lands at or above the design's own E3 fail line
findings.detail: The current 90-rule kernel spec is ~57KB (~15k tokens) for the WHOLE language. Recount the form table honestly: vec needs ~14 op rows (new/push/pop/index/index_uniq/reserve/try_reserve/truncate/extend/as_slice/as_slice_uniq/move-out/iter/iter_uniq), map ~12 plus the entry sub-protocol (Occupied/Vacant are new types with their own ops), file ~12 (two open modes, fill_buf/consume, O_DIRECT alignment preconditions, three sync escapes, replace_atomic), net ~15, hashk ~10 (4 algorithms x one-shot/begin/update/finish). Across 30 forms that is ~250-300 op rows, each needing signature + ownership modes + effect row + trap-vs-Result-vs-Option failure row + postcondition-fact row + fact-INVALIDATION row (which &uniq ops kill the sortedness fact — the design's own risk list admits this must be airtight, i.e., normative). Add: happens-before prose for 7 concurrency forms (publication at join/send/pubcell is a memory model, however small — 1-3k tokens to survive hostile soundness review), the view layout grammar, the classify64 stage algebra (a second language: stage arities, widths, carry-threading rules), the extern per-arg contract vocabulary, and 9 pattern cards that are 'complete copy-adapt programs' — in ANF with no expression nesting and match-only control, an LRU or timer-wheel card is realistically 150-400 lines, so 9 cards alone are ~10-25k tokens. Sum: ~30-50k tokens ON TOP of the 15k kernel = 45-65k, straddling or exceeding the design's own E3 FAIL threshold (>60k). Evidence to settle: author the full appendix + cards and count tokens (E3 step 1) BEFORE any kernel work; my prediction is >60k on the first honest draft, forcing the catalog cut E3 only treats as a contingency.

findings.title: The form APIs as written do not type-check in the existing kernel: Option<&V> and Occupied(&uniq V) put borrows inside enum payloads, which the language forbids
findings.detail: The stance claims 'no new ownership machinery,' but its own source shapes contradict the kernel's no-borrows-in-data rule. map_get -> Option<&V>, pool_get_uniq -> Option<&uniq T>, iter_next -> Option<&T>, and match map_entry { Occupied(v) => ... } all construct enum VALUES carrying borrows — data holding borrows, exactly what OWN-class rules reject and what makes sendable(T) 'nearly free.' Either (a) a new rule class is added — statement/region-scoped borrow-carrying results that may only be immediately matched, never bound or stored — which IS new ownership machinery and several more spec rules with subtle escape conditions (what if the match arm calls another form op on the same container? the Occupied borrow and the new minted borrow alias), or (b) the APIs become continuation-shaped, which needs closures the language deliberately lacks, or (c) they become two-call check-then-access shapes (contains + index), which reintroduces the double-probe cost the entry API exists to kill (one hash, one probe — the demand map calls it 'critical'). Falsification: attempt to type map_entry's source_shape in the current 90-rule checker; it fails today, and the fix's rule count and aliasing corner cases should be written down before the 36-rule claim is repeated.

findings.title: Failure-channel soup: trap vs Result vs Option assigned per-op, violating one-spelling-per-meaning where weak writers are weakest
findings.detail: The catalog mixes three failure channels with no derivable principle: vec index OOB traps, but pool_get on a stale handle returns None; cursor_take_varint returns Result, spsc_push returns Result(Full), chan_recv_block returns 'None(Closed)' (the digest's own shape is ambiguous between Option and Result!); mmap external truncation traps; file errors 'are Result values, never traps' — yet file_read into an iobuf presumably can still trap on a bad iobuf state. A mid-tier model must memorize ~300 per-op failure channels rather than apply a rule, and the failure modes are exactly where W1 writers already err (forgetting the Full arm on spsc_push under backpressure is a liveness bug, not a compile error). R3 says one spelling per meaning; 'this operation can fail' currently has three spellings distributed by historical precedent (Rust's choices) rather than by a stated principle like 'programmer error traps, environmental failure returns.' Evidence: state the principle in one sentence and check all ~300 rows against it — I predict a dozen violations in the digest alone; then measure first-green failure-arm errors in E3 trials as the behavioral confirmation.

findings.title: No mutate-during-iteration ops exist, and the no-reborrow/frozen-iterator rules make the most common maintenance loops (retain/drain/merge) command-buffer detours
findings.detail: Form iterators freeze the container until their region ends, and there is no retain/drain/extract_if/pop-while row anywhere in the 30 forms. Consider the bread-and-butter server loop: 'remove all expired sessions from map' or 'sweep dead entries from pool.' The blessed answer is: iterate collecting doomed keys into a scratch vec, end the iterator region, then a second pass of removes — 2x probes, an allocation, and a shape a weak writer must DISCOVER (the command-buffer card generalized to containers) because the direct spelling is rejected. The timer-wheel card has this internally: firing a bucket means iterating pool-linked entries while unlinking them — expressible only because handles are Copy data and the card carefully never holds an iterator, i.e., the card works around a hole the spec never names. The D9a evidence (percent-encoding, utf8parse) is flat byte kernels with zero container composition, so 'weak writers succeed' has literally no evidence in the regime where this hazard lives. Falsification: add a 'sweep expired entries' task to E3; predict first-green under 50% without a dedicated retain op or an explicit two-pass card, and near-par only if retain rows are added to map/pool/vec/omap (≈8+ more op rows, feeding finding 1).

findings.title: Extensibility cliff, named: a concurrent ordered map (LSM memtable) is outside the catalog and the fallback is a 5-10x performance trap
findings.detail: The demand map itself flags 'concurrent ordered maps, e.g. skiplists in LSM engines' as a real split. The catalog has omap (not sync) and snapmap (not ordered); their product is empty. A writer building a RocksDB-class memtable — squarely 'general systems programming' under the new ruling — has exactly one expressible answer: mutex<omap>, which serializes every reader and writer and loses ~5-10x to a crossbeam-skiplist-class structure at 8 threads on mixed insert/scan. Because epoch reclamation and raw fences are deliberately unrepresentable outside the TCB, the writer CANNOT build a skiplist even badly; the only remedy is the project-side finding queue, whose latency (design a form, verify per the 5-part story on 2 ISAs, hostile review, ship a spec release) is months per finding. Same cliff, second instance: closed derived-Ord means a case-insensitive or locale-collated omap key forces materializing folded key copies (allocation + ~2x key memory) where Rust passes a comparator — a permanent tax, not a queue-latency one. Evidence: write the memtable in catalog forms and benchmark mutex<omap> vs crossbeam-skiplist at 4/8/16 threads; if the gap is the predicted 5-10x, the stance must either add a form (catalog growth pressure, finding 7) or concede a named non-goal in the general-systems claim.

findings.title: classify64 is a second language whose writer-facing value is speculative but whose spec and teaching cost is certain — the worst tokens-per-successful-use entry in the catalog
findings.detail: The stage algebra (nibble shuffle tables, range/eq compares, saturating arithmetic, clmul carry-across-blocks, movemask emit) requires thinking in SIMD dataflow with correct table construction — the precise skill mid-tier models most reliably lack; even for expert humans, deriving the two nibble tables for a new byte-class is the hard part of simdjson-style kernels (Langdale/Lemire spent a paper on it). The four shipped instances (utf8, json_structural, base64, ws_skip) already cover the demand map's F5-2 scenarios, so the schema exists for writer-authored FUTURE formats — yet the design's own risk list admits the algebra was 'reverse-engineered from utf8/JSON/base64' and may not fit the next format, in which case every new kernel is a TCB request anyway and the schema paid its ~10-15 rules of grammar/arity/carry semantics for nothing. Falsification: E3-style trial — give a sonnet-class model the schema spec and ask for a CSV quote-state classifier or a UTF-16 validator, 10 trials; predict <3 first-green. If so, ship classify64 instances as named TCB kernels (like bytescan) and delete the writer-facing schema: same coverage, ~10 fewer rules, one less language to teach.

findings.title: Evolution is unpriced: a normative frozen form table + in-context teaching means every op addition is a breaking spec release, and the base rate of additions is dozens per form per decade
findings.detail: The catalog 'grows only by project releases,' and the spec IS the writers' context (D2: models are prompted from it). Base rate from the references the design itself pins: Rust Vec gained retain_mut, extract_if, drain, dedup_by, spare_capacity_mut, split_off, leak... over ~8 years; hashbrown's API grew entry_ref, get_many_mut, extract_if; every one was demand-driven by real programs. Under this design each such addition is: a form-table row change (normative spec bump) + possible new fact rows + re-run of the per-form verification story + invalidation of every deployed writer's taught context and every pattern card that could now be written better. There is no stated versioning discipline: can a program written against table v1.3 be checked by a v1.4 toolchain? Do postcondition facts ever change meaning? Worse, the finding-queue is the ONLY relief valve for findings 4/5/6, so catalog churn is not hypothetical — it is the design's own maintenance loop, and each turn of it fights D2. Evidence to settle: write the form-table versioning/compatibility rule now and count the last 24 months of xlang's own decision-gate churn as the internal base rate; if no compatibility story fits in a page, the 'closed catalog' is actually an open catalog with a slow, spec-breaking release process.

findings.title: Three byte buffers and silent wrong-form choices: the failure mode is invisible performance loss, which no diagnostic catches and which defeats the catalog's whole purpose
findings.detail: vec<u8>, iobuf, and marginbuf are three owned byte sequences with overlapping roles and boundary conversions (iobuf freeze -> vec<u8>, marginbuf freeze -> vec<u8>); a proxy that reads (iobuf), transforms (marginbuf), and responds (vec<u8> + bytefmt) crosses all three, and a weak writer will plausibly do everything in vec<u8>, paying the memset iobuf exists to kill (20-50% of small-read syscall cost) — compiling green, running slow. Same pattern at higher stakes: chan where spsc fits (5-10x), map where the INLINE knob was needed (3-4x on tiny-map churn), heap where the timer-wheel card was needed (log n vs O(1) at 1M timers). The design calls 'wrong-but-safe' its virtue, but under the owner's P0 ruling the catalog exists to DELIVER top-tier performance through modest writers; if form choice is where the performance actually lives, W1 must be tested on form CHOICE, not just form USE. Evidence: E3 currently grades first-green correctness; add a preregistered form-choice accuracy metric (was iobuf used for the read? spsc for the two-thread stream?) and a perf assertion per task; predict correctness-green with form-choice accuracy near 60-70%, i.e., green programs 2-5x off par — which is the exact failure the stance was adopted to prevent.

overall: The stance is rescuable — TCB-verified builtin forms behind a normative table is the right architecture for this language's constraints, and nothing here breaks the safety or performance story — but it is currently sized and specified dishonestly, and the single change that matters most is to run the E3 spec-authoring step FIRST and let its token count force the catalog down: write the full form table with every op row, every failure row, every fact-invalidation row, the concurrency happens-before prose, and the 9 cards in real ANF, before implementing anything. My recount says that draft lands at 45-65k tokens against a 15k kernel (finding 1), which mandates cutting to ~18-22 forms — the natural cuts are the writer-facing classify64 schema (finding 6), deque-as-vec-knob, and merging the three byte buffers' surface — and the same pass will surface the two design holes that are currently papered over: borrow-carrying results do not type in the existing kernel and need one honestly-admitted new ownership rule (finding 2), and the catalog lacks mutate-during-iteration ops entirely (finding 4). Findings 5 and 7 (the concurrent-ordered-map cliff and the unpriced evolution loop) do not kill the stance but must be named as governed non-goals with a written versioning rule, or the closed catalog quietly becomes an open one with a months-long release valve.
